content:
7 min read
1 hour ago

--

TypeScript 7 went generally available on July 8, 2026. The pitch is simple: the compiler is now written in Go instead of JavaScript, and it runs 8 to 12 times faster on large codebases. Microsoft’s own numbers show the VS Code repo, about 1.5 million lines of code, dropping from 77.8 seconds to 7.5 seconds on a full check.
That part of the story is true, and the forums back it up. But I spent time digging through GitHub issues, Hacker News threads, Reddit comments, and migration write-ups from people who actually flipped the switch, and the picture is more complicated than “just install it and enjoy the speed.” A lot of things break, some quietly and some loudly. Here is what I found.
The compiler API is gone, and that’s the big one
For over a decade, import * as ts from 'typescript' gave you access to the full TypeScript compiler API. People call this the Strada API now, to tell it apart from the new one. Tools like ts-morph, custom AST transformers, codegen scripts, and ts-patch all depend on it.
tsgo does not have it. Microsoft has said plainly that a stable programmatic API is not coming until TypeScript 7.1, several months after the 7.0 release. One developer who tested fifteen popular tools against the native preview found that ts-morph breaks completely, because every one of its calls maps to Strada functions that no longer exist. There is no workaround for that one. Tools like tsup’s declaration generation and some ts-jest setups fail the same way, unless you keep TypeScript 6 installed separately for those specific jobs.
If your team writes internal codegen scripts, custom lint rules, or build plugins that reach into the compiler, this is the change to plan around first.
Linting takes a real hit
typescript-eslint does not work with tsgo out of the box. The typescript-eslint team built an early proof of concept called tsgolint to see how much faster type-aware linting could be on top of the Go compiler, and the results were dramatic, on the order of 20 to 40 times faster than ESLint with typescript-eslint. But the team decided not to put ongoing engineering time into it, since their plan is to keep supporting typed linting through the existing ESLint path.
The Oxc project picked up that early work, with permission, and built it into Oxlint as oxlint-tsgolint. As of now it covers 59 of the 61 type-aware rules from typescript-eslint. The catch is that using it means moving your linter from ESLint to Oxlint, which is a bigger change than a version bump for most teams. If you stay on ESLint and typescript-eslint, you keep your current rules, but you do not get the speed win on the linting side, only on tsc itself.
Framework tooling that leans on the same API
Vue, Svelte, Astro, MDX, and Angular templates all use the compiler API under the hood for their language tooling. Since that API does not exist yet in tsgo, editor support and template checking for these frameworks is not fully caught up. The common workaround people are sharing is installing a compatibility package that exposes a tsc6 command, so framework tooling can keep calling the old API while tsc itself runs the native compiler for everything else. It works, but it means running two compilers side by side for a while, which is exactly the kind of complexity most teams upgrade to get away from.
Config flags that are now hard errors
TypeScript 6.0 was billed as the bridge release. It warned you about anything that would break in 7. In 7.0, those warnings became hard errors, with almost no escape hatch. The changes people keep running into:

• target: "es5" is removed. The floor is now ES2015. If you still need ES5 output, you need Babel or another transpiler in front of tsc.

• module: "amd", "umd", "systemjs", and "none" are gone. Supported values are ESNext, ES2022, NodeNext, and CommonJS.

• moduleResolution: "node10" is gone. You need "bundler" or "nodenext" instead.

• Leaving types unset in tsconfig used to auto-include every @types/* package in node_modules. Now it defaults to an empty array. If your code relies on ambient types from a package you never explicitly listed, expect a wave of new errors until you add it to types or set up typeRoots.

• strict is on by default, module defaults to esnext, and stableTypeOrdering is on with no way to turn it off.
None of this is new information exactly, since TS 6 warned about most of it. But plenty of teams treat deprecation warnings as noise, and this is the release where the noise turns into a broken build.
Decorators and reflect-metadata libraries
experimentalDecorators and emitDecoratorMetadata did get ported to tsgo, so code using the legacy decorator pattern with libraries like TypeORM, NestJS, InversifyJS, or typegoose should still emit working output. But there's an open discussion on the typescript-go repo asking what the long-term plan is for emitDecoratorMetadata, since it was never really part of the standard TC39 decorators design and isn't supported by esbuild, Babel, or Node's own type-stripping. If your app depends heavily on reflect-metadata for runtime dependency injection, it is worth testing this path specifically rather than assuming it just works because your build finishes without errors.
Real crashes on real codebases
This isn’t only about configuration. Some codebases hit actual compiler panics. One open issue on the typescript-go repo shows tsgo failing to compile webpack’s own source, throwing a Go panic about an unexpected node kind during type inference. It’s a reminder that a rewrite this large, even one aimed at being a faithful port rather than a redesign, still has edge cases the test suite hasn’t caught yet, especially on large, older codebases with unusual patterns.
Editor quirks in monorepos
A few issues on the typescript-go tracker are specifically about editor behavior in workspaces with multiple packages. One report describes autoimport suggesting a relative path into a sibling package instead of the package’s proper export, even when the package.json exports field is set up correctly. Another describes autocomplete trying to pull in @types packages that the new, stricter types default should have excluded. These are the kind of paper cuts that don't break your build but do slow down your day, and they show up more in monorepos than single-package projects.
On the positive side, one team documented that switching to tsgo forced them to fix three stale path aliases that had been silently working by accident under the old compiler. They called it a feature, not a bug, since the stricter resolution caught real problems in their setup.
A subtle one: template literal types and Unicode
TypeScript 7 changed how template literal type inference handles Unicode surrogate pairs. Previously, an emoji like the grinning face character would get split into two UTF-16 code units when TypeScript inferred a template literal type, technically consistent with how JavaScript indexes strings, but rarely what anyone actually wanted. Now it is treated as one unit, matching how for...of or the spread operator handles it. Microsoft calls this out directly as a breaking change for any type-level string manipulation that intentionally depended on the old UTF-16 splitting behavior, such as certain string-length utility types. It's a narrow case, but if you have type-level string gymnastics in your codebase, check this one specifically.
Pure JavaScript projects using JSDoc
If you have a JS-only codebase that leans on TypeScript’s JSDoc-based type inference for checking, take note. TypeScript 7 rewrites how .js files are handled, moving away from the old Closure-style JSDoc support toward patterns that behave more like .ts files. People who tested this early found it flew under the radar compared to the more obvious config changes, so it's worth checking your project's CHANGES notes if JSDoc inference is doing real work for you.
What actually held up fine
It’s not all breakage. The parts of TypeScript people worried most about turned out to be solid:

• Project references and --build mode work the same way, just faster, and teams report multi-package CI builds dropping from double digit minutes to a few minutes.

• Incremental builds still work.

• --watch mode was rebuilt from scratch on a Go port of Parcel's file watcher, replacing the old polling approach, and early reports describe it as stable across platforms.

• The core type-checking logic is, by Microsoft’s own description, structurally identical to TypeScript 6.0. It was ported line by line rather than redesigned, specifically so that code compiling correctly under six would keep compiling the same way under seven.
The migration pattern people are actually using
Across the write-ups and forum threads, the same rough sequence keeps showing up:

• Upgrade to TypeScript 6 first and clear every deprecation warning. This alone catches most of what would otherwise break in 7.

• Install the native compiler alongside your existing setup rather than replacing it immediately, so tools that need the old API keep working.

• Run the old and new compilers side by side in CI and compare output before fully switching over.

• Once outputs match and the pipeline is green, point your build and typecheck scripts at the native compiler.

• If you maintain a plugin, transformer, or any tool that imports from typescript directly, keep it pinned to version 6 and wait for the 7.1 API before touching that code.
The bottom line
TypeScript 7 is a genuine engineering achievement, and the speed gains are not marketing fluff. Multiple independent benchmarks from VS Code, Sentry, Bluesky, Playwright, and tldraw all land in the same 7x to 12x range. For type-checking in CI and day to day editor use, most people can adopt it right now with low risk.
Where it gets messy is anything that depends on the compiler as a programmatic tool rather than a command line checker: linters, codegen, custom transformers, and framework language plugins. If your workflow is “write TypeScript, run tsc, ship JavaScript,” this upgrade is close to a free win once you clear TypeScript 6’s deprecation warnings. If your workflow leans on the compiler API anywhere, plan on running two versions side by side until TypeScript 7.1 ships a stable replacement.
